ui.bank_tip: Pick a question. Read the prompt and examples. Solve using a dictionary whenever useful.
ui.editor_tip: Paste starter code into the editor. Test carefully on edge cases.

questions.title: Character Frequency Count
questions.prompt:
Given a string, count the frequency of each character.

Rules:
- Treat uppercase and lowercase as different characters
- Include digits and symbols if present
- Return a dictionary where keys are characters and values are counts

This is one of the most important starter problems for mastering dictionaries.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 760 220" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Character frequency diagram"><rect x="0" y="0" width="760" height="220" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.68)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="38" font-size="17" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Frequency Counting</text><text x="24" y="62" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Input: banana</text><rect x="34" y="86" width="300" height="90" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.12)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="54" y="116" font-size="14" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Read one character at a time</text><text x="54" y="144" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">b → a → n → a → n → a</text><rect x="410" y="86" width="300" height="90" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.82)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="430" y="116" font-size="14" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Dictionary updates</text><text x="430" y="144" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">{'b':1, 'a':3, 'n':2}</text></svg>
hints: Use a dictionary.
If character not present, start its count at 0.
Increment count for every character.
Return the dictionary at the end.

questions.title: First Non-Repeating Character
questions.prompt:
Given a string, find the first character that appears exactly once.

Return that character.
If no such character exists, return None.

This is a very common hiring problem because it tests both counting and ordered scanning.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 760 220" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="First non repeating character"><rect x="0" y="0" width="760" height="220" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.68)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="38" font-size="17" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">First Non-Repeating Character</text><text x="24" y="62" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Step 1: count all characters</text><text x="24" y="82" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Step 2: scan original string again</text><rect x="36" y="102" width="688" height="72" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.10)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="56" y="132" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">a a b b c c d x e</text><text x="56" y="158" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">First count==1 character is d</text></svg>
hints: First pass: build frequency dictionary.
Second pass: scan the original string.
Return the first character whose count is 1.
If none found, return None.
questions.starter_code:

    # TODO
    return None

print(first_non_repeating("aabbccdxe"))
print(first_non_repeating("aabbcc"))

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.prompt:
Given an array of integers and a target value, return the indices of two numbers whose sum equals the target.

Rules:
- Return indices as a tuple (i, j)
- If no pair exists, return None
- You may assume at most one valid answer

This is one of the most famous hiring problems based on hash maps.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 760 240" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two sum dictionary lookup"><rect x="0" y="0" width="760" height="240" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.68)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="38" font-size="17" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Two Sum</text><text x="24" y="62" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">For each value x, check if target-x was seen earlier</text><rect x="36" y="88" width="310" height="112" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.10)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="56" y="118" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">nums = [2, 7, 11, 15]</text><text x="56" y="144" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">target = 9</text><text x="56" y="170" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">At 7, needed value is 2</text><rect x="414" y="88" width="310" height="112" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.82)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="434" y="118" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Seen dictionary</text><text x="434" y="144" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">{2: 0}</text><text x="434" y="170" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Answer = (0, 1)</text></svg>
hints: Use a dictionary to store numbers you have already seen.
For each number x, compute needed = target - x.
If needed is already in dictionary, return its index and current index.
Otherwise store current number and index.

print(two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9))
print(two_sum([3, 2, 4], 6))

questions.title: Valid Anagram
questions.prompt:
Given two strings s and t, determine whether t is an anagram of s.

An anagram uses the same characters with the same frequencies.

Return True if they are anagrams, otherwise False.

This is a classic interview problem because it tests counting and comparison.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 760 220" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Anagram check"><rect x="0" y="0" width="760" height="220" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.68)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="38" font-size="17" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Valid Anagram</text><rect x="36" y="78" width="290" height="100" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.10)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="56" y="110" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">listen</text><text x="56" y="138" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">{l:1, i:1, s:1, t:1, e:1, n:1}</text><rect x="430" y="78" width="290" height="100" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.82)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="450" y="110" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">silent</text><text x="450" y="138" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">same counts → True</text></svg>
hints: If lengths are different, answer is False.
Build frequency count for first string.
Build frequency count for second string.
Compare both dictionaries.

print(is_anagram("listen", "silent"))
print(is_anagram("hello", "world"))

questions.title: Group Anagrams
questions.prompt:
Given a list of words, group the anagrams together.

Return a list of groups.
Words belong to the same group if they contain the same letters with the same frequencies.

This is a high-value interview problem because it combines hashing, canonical representation, and grouping.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 760 250" width="100%" height="auto" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Group anagrams"><rect x="0" y="0" width="760" height="250" rx="18" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.68)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="24" y="38" font-size="17" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Group Anagrams</text><text x="24" y="62" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Use a canonical key such as sorted letters</text><rect x="34" y="90" width="690" height="118" rx="14" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.10)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="54" y="122" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">eat → aet</text><text x="54" y="148" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">tea → aet</text><text x="54" y="174" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">ate → aet</text><text x="280" y="122" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">tan → ant</text><text x="280" y="148" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">nat → ant</text><text x="510" y="122" font-size="13" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">bat → abt</text></svg>
hints: Words that are anagrams have the same sorted form.
Use sorted(word) to build a key.
Store a list of words for each key in a dictionary.
Return all dictionary values at the end.
